title: Willow heart wreath
subtitle: A bent willow rod wreath in a heart form for seasonal decoration
excerpt: A willow heart wreath takes one bundle of fresh or soaked willow rods and 30 minutes: bend the rods into a heart shape, bind them together, and add a hanging loop. A project for any skill level; the flexibility of green willow means even irregular bends produce a natural-looking result. The shape holds permanently as the willow dries.
sourceNotes: Traditional British decorative willow-bending; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. II for bent-rod wreath construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A basket willow species (Salix viminalis or S. purpurea) cultivated specifically for long, flexible rods. The standard commercial basketry willow.
Freshly cut timber still high in moisture content. Freshly cut osier bends without cracking; dried osier must be soaked before bending.
Wrapping one or more thin rods around a bundle or form to hold the shape while it dries.

content.text: A bent willow heart works because
osier

willow; fresh or freshly soaked; will hold any shape it is bent into as it dries. Bend a bundle of rods into a heart form, bind the crossing points, and leave to dry: the willow sets solid within 3 days and holds the heart shape permanently. No frame, no armature, no joinery required.
The willow
Use fresh
green wood

willow rods if available; freshly cut osier in winter bends without preparation. If using dry brown or buff willow, soak for 1 hour before bending. Select long, thin rods: 90 to 120 cm and 4 to 6 mm diameter. You will need 6 to 8 rods of equal length for a 30 cm heart. Thicker rods produce a chunkier heart; thinner rods a more delicate one, but the thin rods must be bent with more care to avoid snapping.
Method
The heart is formed by
binding
a bundle of rods at the top and bottom of the heart, then bending each arm of the bundle into the two lobes.

Gather 6 to 8 rods in a bundle and tie them together at the midpoint with a 60 cm length of split willow or strong twine. This midpoint becomes the top centre of the heart; the dip between the two lobes.

Bend the left-hand group of rods (half the bundle) into the left lobe: curve the rods gently downward from the top tie, forming a smooth arc that comes back to meet the bottom point. Hold the arc in shape with one hand. Bind the bottom point where the two arcs meet with a length of thin willow; 8 to 10 wraps, pulled tight, then tuck the end under the last wrap.

Bend the right-hand group of rods into the right lobe symmetrically. The two lobes should be equal in diameter; lay the heart on a flat surface after forming both lobes and compare the widths before binding the bottom point permanently.

Trim the rod ends at the top and bottom points with the sloyd knife. At the top, leave 4 to 5 cm of the rod ends above the top tie; these can be trimmed to a neat point or bent over to create a decorative tuft. At the bottom, trim close.
Form the hanging loop: take one thin rod and loop it through the top binding, twist the ends together twice, and trim. Or thread a length of raffia or twine through the top binding.

Leave to dry hanging for 3 days. The rods will harden and lighten in colour as they dry. The heart shape sets permanently; the binding is just to hold the shape during drying and can be unwrapped after the willow has dried if a cleaner look is preferred.
Finishing

Leave natural; the pale gold or brown of dried willow is part of the decoration. Decorations can be woven through the finished heart: dried flowers, sprigs of rosemary or lavender, ribbon. A light misting of water once a year prevents the willow from becoming brittle in a dry room.
Variations
Wreath circle: bend the bundle into a full circle rather than a heart. The binding point is a single spot at the overlap where the rod ends meet the starting point.
Star: divide the bundle into 5 groups and bind them into 5 arms at even angles. Each arm bends into a point.
White willow heart: soak white (stripped) willow for 1 hour, bend as above. The pale cream colour gives a cleaner, more contemporary result than natural brown.
Care
Keep dry. Mist annually if the willow becomes very brittle. Replace the hanging cord when it perishes but the willow itself will last for many years in indoor conditions.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the willow heart wreath.

items.symptom: Rods snap when bending the lobe arcs
items.cause: Rods too dry or bent too sharply at the top fold
items.fix: Soak dry rods for at least 1 hour and mellow under a damp cloth for 30 minutes. Bend gradually over 5 to 10 seconds rather than sharply.

items.symptom: Lobes unequal in size after drying
items.cause: Unequal rod lengths in each group, or one lobe bent tighter than the other
items.fix: Before the willow dries, loosen the bottom binding and re-form the smaller lobe to match the larger one. Bind again and re-hang.

items.symptom: Heart loses shape after drying; lobes splay outward
items.cause: Binding not pulled tight enough before the willow set
items.fix: A misting of water will re-flex the dried rods slightly. Re-bind tighter while the willow is damp, then leave to re-dry hanging.
